judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently reach the same correct root cause, commit, disagreement rationale, and recommendation, hitting all four expectations; Report 2 is marginally more explicit about avoiding the shared nightly runner and proposing an isolated verification method.

report_1.evidence: It still says `expected_label_field: label`, but commit `53551e8` renamed the gold-label field in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` from `label` to `gold_label`. In `pipeline/score_batch.py:42-48`, the scorer does `gold = record.get(gold_field)` with `gold_field = "label"`; since no record has a `label` key anymore, `.get()` silently returns `None` for every record... The fix is `expected_label_field: gold_label`.

report_2.evidence: It still says `expected_label_field: label`, but the eval set `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` was migrated so every record's gold-label key is now `gold_label`. The batch scorer (`pipeline/score_batch.py:41-47`) does `gold = record.get(config["expected_label_field"])`, i.e. `record.get("label")`, which returns `None` for every record... The fix is a one-line change: `expected_label_field: gold_label`.

report_1.evidence: the regression began with the nightly run of **2026-06-17**, introduced by commit **`53551e8` "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"**... Evidence: `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and 0.00 on 2026-06-17 and every night since; `git show 53551e8` shows it renamed the field in all 11 eval records and in `score_batch.py`'s `REQUIRED_KEYS` while leaving `config/scoring_config.yaml` on the old field name.

report_2.evidence: Commit `53551e8` — "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC. Its diff renames `label` → `gold_label` in all 11 fixture records and updates `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py`, but leaves `config/scoring_config.yaml` untouched... `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows 0.91/0.90/0.92 through 2026-06-16 and 0.00 from 2026-06-17 onward, the same day as the commit

report_1.evidence: it re-ran tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`, which only calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` — it never loads the config, never reads the gold label, and never computes accuracy. So "predictions look correct" was true and irrelevant... Correct reproduction: run the actual batch scorer (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`) and observe 0.00

report_2.evidence: `pipeline/score_one.py` calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` directly and never loads the config or compares against a gold label, so it exercises only the prediction half of the pipeline, which was never broken... Correct reproduction: run the batch scorer itself (accuracy=0.00)

report_1.evidence: The proposed next step — "switch the nightly runner back to the previous model" — should not be acted on. It cannot help: there is no model in the loop... The predictions were never wrong, so changing what produces them cannot fix a scoring-side field mismatch; accuracy would remain 0.0 after a rollback, wasting time... The correct action is the one-line config fix in answer 1.

report_2.evidence: flipping a model/config on the shared nightly job just to observe the effect is exactly the "flip it to see what happens in a shared environment" anti-pattern; the correct experiment is the one-line in-memory check above, which already proves the fix... even if a model swap did something, the defect is in the gold-label lookup, so accuracy would remain 0.00 under any model
